  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The finding is a false positive for CWE-078. The reported sink is org.apache.tika.parser.external2.ExternalParser, which builds a fixed argument list and executes it with new ProcessBuilder(thisCommandLine). The command itself comes from parser configuration via setCommandLine(List<String>), not from request data. For the server entry points in the report, the only realistic attacker influence that can reach the sink is the input file name metadata used to derive the temporary file path for ${INPUT_FILE}; that value is not interpreted by a shell, because ProcessUtils.execute calls pb.start() directly. In addition, the request-derived file name is reduced to a safe extension by TikaInputStream.getExtension(metadata) and FilenameUtils.getSuffixFromPath, which only accepts a suffix matching \\.[a-z0-9]{1,5}. So the actual argv element becomes a temp path with fixed prefix apache-tika- and an optional short alphanumeric extension, not attacker-controlled command text. Several reported sources such as the field and handlerTypeName path parameters do not affect the command line at all, which shows CodeQL over-approximated taint through the parse pipeline. The exact external2.ExternalParser sink is also not part of the default server parser service registration in this tree and appears only in test/custom config paths, which further limits default reachability.",
  "FP_Reason": ["B1"],
  "evidence": "Key code path: ExternalParser.parse substitutes ${INPUT_FILE} with ProcessUtils.escapeCommandLine(p.toAbsolutePath().toString()) and then executes ProcessUtils.execute(new ProcessBuilder(thisCommandLine), ...) at org/apache/tika/parser/external2/ExternalParser.java:112-135. ProcessUtils.execute starts the process with pb.start() at org/apache/tika/utils/ProcessUtils.java:87-95, so no shell command string is evaluated. The request file name enters metadata through TikaResource.detectFilename and fillMetadata at org/apache/tika/server/core/resource/TikaResource.java:141-157 and 303-307. That metadata is converted only to a suffix in TikaInputStream.getExtension at org/apache/tika/io/TikaInputStream.java:513-518, and FilenameUtils.getSuffixFromPath only returns extensions matching \\.[a-z0-9]{1,5} at org/apache/tika/io/FilenameUtils.java:121-131. TikaInputStream.getPath then creates a temp file via tmp.createTempFile(suffix) at org/apache/tika/io/TikaInputStream.java:732, and TemporaryResources.createTempFile uses fixed prefix apache-tika- at org/apache/tika/io/TemporaryResources.java:86-90. Therefore attacker-controlled metacharacters, separators, or a leading option string cannot reach the spawned argv. Also, the reported sources at MetadataResource.java:97 and RecursiveMetadataResource.java:169 / TikaResource.java:566 are only path parameters for field/handler selection and are not used in thisCommandLine. Finally, the default server parser service file registers org.apache.tika.parser.external.CompositeExternalParser, not external2.ExternalParser, at tika-server/tika-server-standard/src/main/resources/META-INF/services/org.apache.tika.parser.Parser:16, while external2.ExternalParser appears in this tree only in test configs such as tika-core/src/test/resources/org/apache/tika/config/TIKA-3557.xml:20-28.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}

Location-file: /tika-core/src/main/java/org/apache/tika/parser/external/ExternalParser.java
Location-start-line: 312
Location-start-col: 53
Location-end-line: 312
Location-end-col: 58",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The report is a false positive. The sink is an external-process launch in `ExternalParser`, but the request-controlled values from the server endpoints do not reach that launch as raw command text. `ExternalParser` substitutes `${INPUT}` with `stream.getFile().getPath()`, and for uploaded or URL-backed streams that path is a locally generated temporary file path. The only request-derived part that can influence that temp filename is the resource-name extension, and `TikaInputStream.getExtension()` reduces it to `FilenameUtils.getSuffixFromPath(name)`, which only accepts `.` followed by 1-5 ASCII alphanumerics. `TemporaryResources.createTempFile()` then creates a file named `apache-tika-<random><suffix>`. The process is started with `Runtime.exec(cmd)` or `exec(cmd[0])`, which does not invoke a shell, so shell metacharacters, spaces, and separators are not interpreted. For `TikaInputStream.get(URL)`, non-file URLs also end up in the same temp-file flow; `file://` URLs may preserve an absolute local path, but that still occupies only one argv element and does not let an attacker inject a new command. In real use, the attacker can influence what file is parsed, but not the executable, argument structure, or shell behavior required for CWE-078.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "D1"
  ],
  "evidence": "`tika-core/src/main/java/org/apache/tika/parser/external/ExternalParser.java:296-315` replaces `${INPUT}` with `stream.getFile().getPath()` and then executes the command. `tika-server/tika-server-core/src/main/java/org/apache/tika/server/core/resource/TikaResource.java:303-307` copies the request filename into `RESOURCE_NAME_KEY`. `tika-core/src/main/java/org/apache/tika/io/TikaInputStream.java:513-518` computes the suffix from that metadata via `FilenameUtils.getSuffixFromPath(name)`. `tika-core/src/main/java/org/apache/tika/io/FilenameUtils.java:121-129` only returns a suffix matching `\\.[A-Za-z0-9]{1,5}`; otherwise it returns empty. `tika-core/src/main/java/org/apache/tika/io/TikaInputStream.java:732-752` then creates a fresh temp file with `tmp.createTempFile(suffix)`, and `tika-core/src/main/java/org/apache/tika/io/TemporaryResources.java:79-85` uses `Files.createTempFile(\"apache-tika-\", actualSuffix)`. So the executed argv contains a locally generated path such as `/tmp/apache-tika-12345.pdf`, not raw attacker-controlled command text. The shipped external-parser configs also use fixed commands like `ffmpeg -i ${INPUT}`, `exiftool ${INPUT}`, and `sox --info ${INPUT}`, meaning the user input is only ever the file operand.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}

  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The finding is a false positive. The reported HTTP/URL inputs can reach `GDALParser.parse(...)`, but they do not create a real CWE-078 condition. `GDALParser` uses a fixed command template, `gdalinfo ${INPUT}`, and only substitutes the input file path; the executable itself is not user-controlled through the reported paths. The process is launched with `ProcessBuilder`/`pb.start()`, not through a shell, so shell metacharacters are not interpreted as OS commands. In the normal tika-server upload flow, a user-supplied filename is reduced to a sanitized suffix before the temporary file path is created, so attacker-controlled basename content does not survive into the executed argument. At most, the manual whitespace split could cause parsing problems for unusual file paths, but that is not reliable command execution and does not satisfy CWE-078.",
  "FP_Reason": ["B1"],
  "evidence": "`GDALParser()` hard-codes the command with `setCommand(\"gdalinfo ${INPUT}\")` at `tika-parsers/tika-parsers-extended/tika-parser-scientific-module/src/main/java/org/apache/tika/parser/gdal/GDALParser.java:154-156`. The only substitution is `this.command.replace(INPUT_FILE_TOKEN, tis.getFile().getPath())` at `GDALParser.java:166-172`, and execution is `new ProcessBuilder(runCommand)` at `GDALParser.java:198-201`, which reaches `pb.start()` in `tika-core/src/main/java/org/apache/tika/utils/ProcessUtils.java:87-95`; no shell is involved. On the server path, `TikaResource.fillMetadata()` copies the request filename into `RESOURCE_NAME_KEY` at `tika-server/tika-server-core/src/main/java/org/apache/tika/server/core/resource/TikaResource.java:303-306`, but `TikaInputStream.getExtension()` keeps only `FilenameUtils.getSuffixFromPath(name)` at `tika-core/src/main/java/org/apache/tika/io/TikaInputStream.java:513-518`. `FilenameUtils.getSuffixFromPath()` accepts only extensions matching `\\.[A-Za-z0-9]{1,5}` and otherwise returns empty string at `tika-core/src/main/java/org/apache/tika/io/FilenameUtils.java:121-131`. `TikaInputStream.getPath()` then creates a temp file via `tmp.createTempFile(suffix)` at `TikaInputStream.java:725-733`, and `TemporaryResources.createTempFile()` uses a fixed prefix `apache-tika-` plus that sanitized suffix at `tika-core/src/main/java/org/apache/tika/io/TemporaryResources.java:86-100`. Therefore the reported user input does not become an uncontrolled shell command in real-world execution.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"

3:
{
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The report reaches a real process-execution site in `ExternalParser.parse()`, but it does not describe a real CWE-078 command-injection path. `ExternalParser` executes an administrator-configured command line (`setCommandLine`, `ExternalParser.java:236-238`), not a user-built shell command. During parsing it substitutes `${INPUT_FILE}` / `${OUTPUT_FILE}` with a path and then calls `new ProcessBuilder(thisCommandLine)` (`ExternalParser.java:112-130`). `ProcessUtils.execute()` starts that `ProcessBuilder` directly with `pb.start()` (`ProcessUtils.java:87-95`), so there is no `sh -c` / `cmd /c` shell interpretation. More importantly, the reported user input does not remain arbitrary command text by the time it reaches the sink. The request/URL is converted into a `TikaInputStream`, and `getPath()` creates a server-side temporary file (`TikaInputStream.java:732`). `TemporaryResources.createTempFile()` hardcodes the prefix `apache-tika-` and lets Java generate the random body (`TemporaryResources.java:86-90`). The only user influence that can survive into that filename is the suffix derived from metadata/resource name, and `FilenameUtils.getSuffixFromPath()` only preserves extensions matching `\\A\\.(?i)[a-z0-9]{1,5}\\Z`; otherwise it returns empty (`FilenameUtils.java:121-131`). So the actual argv is a fixed binary plus a generated absolute temp path like `/tmp/apache-tika-<random>.pdf`, not attacker-controlled command syntax. This is not a realistic OS command injection in Tika.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "D1.Missed Key Program Points"
  ],
  "evidence": "Key path: server endpoints such as `MetadataResource.getMetadata()` (`MetadataResource.java:64-68`), `RecursiveMetadataResource.getMetadata()` (`RecursiveMetadataResource.java:169-173`), `UnpackerResource.unpack()` (`UnpackerResource.java:99-100`), and `XMPMetadataResource.getMetadata()` (`XMPMetadataResource.java:60-63`) feed request input into Tika parsing. In the sink method, `ExternalParser.parse()` does `TikaInputStream tis = TikaInputStream.get(stream, tmp, metadata); Path p = tis.getPath(); ... c.replace(INPUT_FILE_TOKEN, ProcessUtils.escapeCommandLine(p.toAbsolutePath().toString())); ... new ProcessBuilder(thisCommandLine)` (`ExternalParser.java:106-130`). The missed sanitizing/normalizing points are: `TikaInputStream.getPath()` creates a new temp file (`TikaInputStream.java:732`); `TemporaryResources.createTempFile()` uses `Files.createTempFile(\"apache-tika-\", actualSuffix)` (`TemporaryResources.java:86-90`); `FilenameUtils.getSuffixFromPath()` only allows a short alphanumeric extension (`FilenameUtils.java:121-128`). Also, `ProcessUtils.execute()` invokes `pb.start()` directly (`ProcessUtils.java:87-95`), so shell metacharacters are not interpreted. Example: even if an attacker supplied a filename like `evil;rm -rf /;.pdf`, Tika would execute something equivalent to `['exiftool', '/tmp/apache-tika-12345.pdf']` or `['file', '-b', '--mime-type', '/tmp/apache-tika-12345.xml']` (see `TIKA-3557*.xml` test configs), not an injected shell command.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}",

Location-file: /tika-parsers/tika-parsers-standard/tika-parsers-standard-modules/tika-parser-ocr-module/src/main/java/org/apache/tika/parser/ocr/TesseractOCRParser.java
Location-start-line: 437
Location-start-col: 48
Location-end-line: 437
Location-end-col: 50",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The reported sink is `new ProcessBuilder(cmd)` in `TesseractOCRParser.doOCR` (`.../TesseractOCRParser.java:417-437`). In this code, the executable is the fixed parser-side value `getTesseractPath() + getTesseractProg()`, while request-time data only contributes separate argv tokens such as the OCR language, page segmentation mode, and internally generated temp file paths. This is not shell execution, so shell metacharacters are not interpreted. More importantly, the request cannot set the executable path: the server populates a per-request `TesseractOCRConfig` object (`TesseractServerConfig.java:51-66`), and the parser only merges `parseContext.get(TesseractOCRConfig.class)` (`TesseractOCRParser.java:246-249`). `TesseractOCRConfig` does not contain `tesseractPath`; that setter exists on `TesseractOCRParser`, not on the request object. The request-reachable string arguments that do reach `cmd` are also validated: `setPageSegMode` only allows `0-13`, `setLanguage` enforces a strict language-code regex, `setPageSeparator` only allows `[-_/\\.A-Z0-9]+`, and `setOutputType` only allows `txt`/`hocr`. The temp input path is internally created with `Files.createTempFile(\"apache-tika-\", suffix)` and the user-controlled filename only affects a short sanitized suffix via `FilenameUtils.getSuffixFromPath`, so it cannot become an injected option or command fragment. Therefore the CodeQL report does not correspond to a real CWE-078 OS command injection.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "B1"
  ],
  "evidence": "1. Sink shape: `Arrays.asList(getTesseractPath() + getTesseractProg(), input.getPath(), output.getPath(), \"--psm\", config.getPageSegMode())` followed by `new ProcessBuilder(cmd)` (`TesseractOCRParser.java:417-437`). This uses tokenized arguments, not `sh -c` / `cmd.exe /c`.\n2. Request config type: `TesseractServerConfig.configure(...)` creates `TesseractOCRConfig ocrConfig` and stores it with `parseContext.set(TesseractOCRConfig.class, ocrConfig)` (`TesseractServerConfig.java:55-66`). The parser later reads exactly that type: `TesseractOCRConfig userConfig = parseContext.get(TesseractOCRConfig.class)` (`TesseractOCRParser.java:246-249`). So remote input cannot reach parser fields like `tesseractPath`.\n3. Server-side guards: `processHeaderConfig` calls `checkTrustWorthy` for string setters and rejects any setter containing `trusted` (`TikaResource.java:239-242`, `267-277`). Tests confirm `X-Tika-OCRtesseractPath` and `X-Tika-OCRtrustedPageSeparator` are rejected with HTTP 400 (`TikaResourceTest.java:474-511`).\n4. Argument validation: `setPageSegMode` only allows `[0-9]|10|11|12|13`; `setLanguage` validates language syntax; `setPageSeparator` enforces `(?i)^[-_/\\.A-Z0-9]+$`; `addOtherTesseractConfig` also validates key/value, and in practice is not header-settable through `TesseractOCRConfig` (`TesseractOCRConfig.java:153-212`, `521-538`).\n5. Temp-file path sanitization: the only filename-derived part is `FilenameUtils.getSuffixFromPath(name)`, which returns only extensions like `.pdf` with 1-5 alphanumerics (`TikaInputStream.java:513-519`, `FilenameUtils.java:113-129`); then `TikaInputStream.getPath()` uses `tmp.createTempFile(suffix)` and `TemporaryResources.createTempFile` uses fixed prefix `apache-tika-` (`TikaInputStream.java:725-733`, `TemporaryResources.java:86-90`).",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}

  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The finding is a false positive. In `tika-core/src/main/java/org/apache/tika/parser/external2/ExternalParser.java`, the executed command is built from the parser's preconfigured `commandLine` list, not from request data. `ExternalParser.parse()` only replaces `${INPUT_FILE}` and `${OUTPUT_FILE}` with paths of server-created temporary files before calling `new ProcessBuilder(thisCommandLine)`. The HTTP values cited by the report are request `InputStream`s or nearby metadata, which let an attacker supply document bytes, but do not let the attacker choose the executable or inject extra command arguments. At most, a request filename or URL path can influence `RESOURCE_NAME_KEY`, but that is reduced to a sanitized suffix and then used only to create a fresh temp file path like `apache-tika-XXXX.pdf`. In the real world, this means a client can cause a configured external parser such as `exiftool` to process attacker-controlled file content, which is intended parser behavior, not CWE-078 command injection. The report therefore conflates attacker-controlled input content/metadata with attacker-controlled command construction.",
  "FP_Reason": ["B1"],
  "evidence": "`ExternalParser.parse()` builds the command only from configuration and temp-file paths: it iterates over `commandLine`, substitutes `${INPUT_FILE}` / `${OUTPUT_FILE}`, and then executes `ProcessUtils.execute(new ProcessBuilder(thisCommandLine), ...)` (`tika-core/src/main/java/org/apache/tika/parser/external2/ExternalParser.java:112-130`). The only setter for that list is `setCommandLine(List<String> commandLine)` (`ExternalParser.java:236-238`), which is loaded from Tika config, not from HTTP requests; the example config uses fixed values `exiftool` and `${INPUT_FILE}` (`tika-core/src/test/resources/org/apache/tika/config/TIKA-3557-exiftool-example.xml:25-27`). Request metadata can only affect the temporary file suffix: `fillMetadata()` copies the request filename into `RESOURCE_NAME_KEY` (`tika-server/tika-server-core/src/main/java/org/apache/tika/server/core/resource/TikaResource.java:303-306`), `TikaInputStream.getExtension()` turns that into `FilenameUtils.getSuffixFromPath(name)` (`tika-core/src/main/java/org/apache/tika/io/TikaInputStream.java:513-518`), `FilenameUtils.getSuffixFromPath()` accepts only `.[A-Za-z0-9]{1,5}` (`tika-core/src/main/java/org/apache/tika/io/FilenameUtils.java:46,121-125`), and `getPath()` / `TemporaryResources.createTempFile()` create a new server temp file with prefix `apache-tika-` (`TikaInputStream.java:725-733`, `TemporaryResources.java:86-90`). Also, execution is `pb.start()` on `ProcessBuilder(List<String>)` (`tika-core/src/main/java/org/apache/tika/utils/ProcessUtils.java:87-95,165-177`), so no shell interprets metacharacters. There is also no per-request `ExternalParser` configuration hook in the server parse-context registrations; the registered request-side configs are PasswordProvider/DocumentSelector/Timeout/PDF/Tesseract, not `ExternalParser`.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}

  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The sink is real (`Runtime.exec(...)` in `ExternalParser`), and the path is reachable when Tika chooses an external parser, so this is not an A1/A2 reachability artifact. However, the reported user input does not become an attacker-controlled OS command. The external command itself is fixed by server-side parser configuration, not by the HTTP request, and `ExternalParser` executes a `String[]` argv via `Runtime.exec(cmd)`, not a shell command string. The request can at most influence the parsed document bytes and, in upload/URL cases, the temporary file suffix. That does not let the attacker inject extra commands, shell metacharacters, or additional argv boundaries. In real use, this code makes Tika run fixed tools such as `ffmpeg`, `exiftool`, or `sox` on attacker-supplied files, but that is not CWE-078 command injection in Tika itself.",
  "FP_Reason": ["B1"],
  "evidence": "1) The command is fixed from server-side XML, not request data: `ExternalParsersConfigReader` calls `parser.setCommand(getString(child))` (`tika-core/.../ExternalParsersConfigReader.java:115-117`), and the bundled config contains constant commands such as `ffmpeg -i ${INPUT}`, `env FOO=${OUTPUT} exiftool ${INPUT}`, and `env FOO=${OUTPUT} sox --info ${INPUT}` (`tika-core/.../tika-external-parsers.xml:29,53,69`). 2) The server entry points only pass the uploaded stream into parsing; the default factory is a passthrough (`DefaultInputStreamFactory.getInputStream` just returns `is`, `tika-server/.../DefaultInputStreamFactory.java:33-35`). 3) If the client supplies a filename, it is not used verbatim in the executed command: `TikaResource.fillMetadata` stores it as `RESOURCE_NAME_KEY` (`tika-server/.../TikaResource.java:303-306`), `TikaInputStream.getExtension` reduces that to a suffix only (`tika-core/.../TikaInputStream.java:513-518`), and `FilenameUtils.getSuffixFromPath` only accepts short alphanumeric extensions (`tika-core/.../FilenameUtils.java:121-129`). 4) When a file is needed, `TikaInputStream.getPath()` creates a server-side temp file with `tmp.createTempFile(suffix)` and copies the stream there (`tika-core/.../TikaInputStream.java:732-748`); `TemporaryResources.createTempFile` uses `Files.createTempFile(\"apache-tika-\", actualSuffix)` (`tika-core/.../TemporaryResources.java:86-90`). So `${INPUT}` becomes a server-generated path like `apache-tika-<random>.ext`, not attacker-controlled command text. 5) `ExternalParser` tokenizes the fixed command first (`cmd = command[0].split(\" \")`), then replaces `${INPUT}` / `${OUTPUT}`, and finally calls `Runtime.exec(cmd)` (`tika-core/.../ExternalParser.java:288-315`). Because replacement happens after tokenization and the sink is `exec(String[])`, shell metacharacters in any filename/path are not interpreted as shell syntax and do not create extra argv elements.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}

  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "This is a false positive for CWE-078. The reported user-controlled data does not become an attacker-controlled command or option. In the server paths, request data only influences the uploaded document stream and, at most, the metadata filename. When `ExternalParser.parse()` needs a file, it wraps the stream in `TikaInputStream` and later substitutes `stream.getFile().getPath()` into the configured command. But that path is not the raw user filename: `TikaInputStream.get(..., metadata)` reduces `RESOURCE_NAME_KEY` to a sanitized suffix via `getExtension(metadata)`, and the actual file path is then created with a fixed prefix and random temp name. As a result, the attacker can at most affect a short extension such as `.pdf`; they cannot inject spaces, shell metacharacters, path separators, or a leading `-` that would alter the executed command line. Also, the exact sink reported at `ExternalParser.java:312` (`exec(cmd[0])`) is only taken when the configured command has a single token; the in-tree external parser configs use multi-token commands like `ffmpeg -i ${INPUT}` and therefore execute via `exec(String[])` at line 314, not the reported branch. In real deployment, this means the request can cause Tika to run an already-configured external parser on the uploaded file, which is intended behavior, but it cannot turn this path into OS command injection.",
  "FP_Reason": ["D1.Missed Key Program Points"],
  "evidence": "1) Request filename enters metadata in `TikaResource.fillMetadata()` (`TikaResource.java:303-307`). 2) That value is narrowed to a suffix in `TikaInputStream.getExtension()` (`TikaInputStream.java:513-519`), which calls `FilenameUtils.getSuffixFromPath()`. 3) `FilenameUtils.getSuffixFromPath()` only returns extensions of the form `.` plus 1-5 alphanumeric characters, otherwise it returns empty (`FilenameUtils.java:46,121-131`); the test `FilenameUtilsTest.java:108-109` shows malformed names lose their suffix. 4) `TikaInputStream.getPath()` creates the backing file with `tmp.createTempFile(suffix)` (`TikaInputStream.java:725-752`), and `TemporaryResources.createTempFile()` uses `Files.createTempFile(\"apache-tika-\", actualSuffix)` (`TemporaryResources.java:86-90`), giving a fixed safe prefix and random temp path. 5) The sink branch in the report is `if (cmd.length == 1) process = Runtime.getRuntime().exec(cmd[0]);` (`ExternalParser.java:290-315`). Shipped configs are multi-token, e.g. `ffmpeg -i ${INPUT}` and `env FOO=${OUTPUT} exiftool ${INPUT}` (`tika-external-parsers.xml:29,53`), so they do not use the reported `exec(cmd[0])` branch. Therefore the report overstates taint reachability to a dangerous command line and misses the sanitizing/narrowing steps that make the path non-exploitable for CWE-078.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}

  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The sink is a real external-process launch, but the reported HTTP/URL sources do not reach it as attacker-controlled command text in Tika's normal flow. GDALParser always starts from the fixed template \"gdalinfo ${INPUT}\". For the server endpoints in the report, the request body is wrapped into a TikaInputStream, and the value later substituted into ${INPUT} is a server-generated temporary file path, not the original user string. User-controlled filename metadata is reduced to a sanitized extension only, then a temp file named like apache-tika-<random>.<ext> is created. ProcessBuilder is invoked directly, not through a shell, so there is no shell metacharacter execution either. The report also cites clearly unrelated request parameters such as /meta/{field} and handlerTypeName, which do not flow into the command construction at all. At most, there is a robustness bug because split(\"\\\\s+\") would mishandle paths with spaces, but that is not attacker-controlled on the reported paths and does not make this report a real CWE-078.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "D1.Missed Key Program Points"
  ],
  "evidence": "Command construction happens in GDALParser: setCommand(\"gdalinfo ${INPUT}\") at lines 154-155, replacement with tis.getFile().getPath() at line 171, split at line 198, and execution via new ProcessBuilder(runCommand) at lines 201-202. The server only copies a request filename into metadata in TikaResource.detectFilename/fillMetadata (TikaResource.java lines 141-157 and 303-306). When a normal request stream is wrapped, TikaInputStream.get(stream, tmp, metadata) stores only getExtension(metadata) (TikaInputStream.java lines 232-245). That extension comes from FilenameUtils.getSuffixFromPath, which only accepts suffixes matching \\A\\.(?i)[a-z0-9]{1,5}\\Z (FilenameUtils.java line 46, and lines 121-128). When GDALParser later asks for a file, TikaInputStream.getPath() spools to tmp.createTempFile(suffix) (TikaInputStream.java lines 725-752), and TemporaryResources.createTempFile creates a file with fixed prefix apache-tika- and server-chosen random name (TemporaryResources.java lines 86-90). So even a malicious uploaded name such as \"foo --config evil\" cannot become extra argv tokens; it collapses to a safe suffix or empty suffix, and the actual command is effectively gdalinfo <server-temp-file>. The report's listed sources also include MetadataResource.getMetadataField(..., String field) at line 97 and RecursiveMetadataResource(..., String handlerTypeName) at line 169, but those parameters are not used in command building, which further shows imprecision. Finally, fetcher-based input streams are not the default server mode: TikaServerProcess uses DefaultInputStreamFactory unless enableUnsecureFeatures is set (TikaServerProcess.java lines 208-212).",
  "new_reason:": ""
}",

Because the process is launched with `ProcessBuilder(List<String>)`, not a shell string, shell metacharacters are not interpreted. The configured argv itself is server-side, e.g. test config shows `<string>exiftool</string><string>${INPUT_FILE}</string>` (`tika-core/src/test/resources/org/apache/tika/config/TIKA-3557-exiftool-example.xml:25-28`). {
  "report": "Rule Name: Executing a command with a relative path
Rule Description: Executing a command with a relative path is vulnerable to malicious changes in the PATH environment variable.
Severity: warning
Detailed Message: Command with a relative path 'unrar' is executed.
Location-file: /tika-parsers/tika-parsers-standard/tika-parsers-standard-modules/tika-parser-pkg-module/src/main/java/org/apache/tika/parser/pkg/UnrarParser.java
Location-start-line: 147
Location-start-col: 17
Location-end-line: 147
Location-end-col: 23",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The reported sink exists, but this is not a real CWE-078 issue under Tika's actual dataflow and deployment model. In `UnrarParser.parse()`, the untrusted archive stream is first copied into a newly created temporary file, then `unrar(cwd, tmp)` is invoked. Inside `unrar()`, the executable name is a fixed literal (`\"unrar\"`), the arguments are fixed flags plus the generated absolute temp-file path, and `ProcessUtils.execute()` starts the process via `pb.start()` rather than through a shell. So the archive input does not control the command name, does not control `PATH`, and does not create a command-injection path. The only way this becomes dangerous is if the runtime environment is already compromised or misconfigured so that `PATH` resolves `unrar` to a malicious binary. That is an external deployment problem, not an attacker-controlled application input flow in Tika. Also, `UnrarParser` is not the default RAR parser in normal Tika operation; it is an opt-in parser selected via explicit `tika-config.xml`, which further reduces practical reachability.",
  "FP_Reason": ["B1"],
  "evidence": "Code path: `UnrarParser.parse()` creates `cwd` with `Files.createTempDirectory(...)` and `tmp` with `Files.createTempFile(cwd, \"input\", \".rar\")`, then calls `unrar(cwd, tmp)` (`UnrarParser.java:77-83`). Sink: `unrar()` builds `ProcessBuilder` with `pb.command(\"unrar\", \"x\", \"-kb\", \"-p-\", ProcessUtils.escapeCommandLine(tmp.toAbsolutePath().toString()))` (`UnrarParser.java:144-154`). Execution: `ProcessUtils.execute()` simply does `p = pb.start()` (`ProcessUtils.java:87-95`), so no shell interprets attacker-controlled metacharacters. Default reachability: the module service file registers only `CompressorParser`, `PackageParser`, and `RarParser`, not `UnrarParser` (`META-INF/services/org.apache.tika.parser.Parser:16-18`). To use `UnrarParser`, the test config must explicitly exclude `RarParser` and add `UnrarParser` (`tika-unrar-config.xml:20-24`). Therefore the finding is based on treating a fixed relative executable path as an exploitable sink even though no attacker-controlled source can influence the resolved executable in Tika's real request/data flow.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}

  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The sink is a real process-execution site (`Runtime.getRuntime().exec(cmd)` in `ExternalParser`), but the reported user input does not control the command line in a way that can trigger CWE-078. In the server flow, a client-supplied filename may be copied into `RESOURCE_NAME_KEY`, but before it is ever used for `${INPUT}`, Tika reduces it to a file extension only, creates a fresh temporary file with a fixed `apache-tika-` prefix, and passes that generated path as a single argument to `Runtime.exec(String[])`. Java is not invoking a shell here, so shell metacharacters are not interpreted. The executable name and options come from trusted parser configuration, not from the request. Some CodeQL-listed sources such as `field` and `handlerTypeName` do not participate in command construction at all. Therefore, even if the external parser is reachable for some MIME types and installed tools, this path does not allow a remote attacker to inject or alter the OS command itself. Any risk from a malicious document exploiting `ffmpeg`/`exiftool`/`sox` would be a separate external-tool vulnerability, not CWE-078 in Tika.",
  "FP_Reason": ["D1"],
  "evidence": "1. Request-controlled filename enters metadata: `TikaResource.detectFilename()` reads `Content-Disposition` / `File-Name` (`tika-server/tika-server-core/src/main/java/org/apache/tika/server/core/resource/TikaResource.java:141-157`), and `fillMetadata()` stores it in `RESOURCE_NAME_KEY` (`:303-306`). 2. That value is sanitized down to an extension only: `TikaInputStream.get(stream, tmp, metadata)` stores `getExtension(metadata)` as the suffix (`tika-core/src/main/java/org/apache/tika/io/TikaInputStream.java:244`); `getExtension()` calls `FilenameUtils.getSuffixFromPath(name)` (`:513-518`); `getSuffixFromPath()` strips path components and only returns a suffix if it is a dot plus 1-5 alphanumeric characters (`tika-core/src/main/java/org/apache/tika/io/FilenameUtils.java:97-129`). The unit test shows malformed names yield an empty suffix (`tika-core/src/test/java/org/apache/tika/io/FilenameUtilsTest.java:107-109`). 3. The actual path used in the command is server-generated: `getPath()` creates `tmp.createTempFile(suffix)` (`TikaInputStream.java:725-733`), and `TemporaryResources.createTempFile()` uses `Files.createTempFile(\"apache-tika-\", actualSuffix)` (`tika-core/src/main/java/org/apache/tika/io/TemporaryResources.java:86-90`). So the command receives something like `/tmp/apache-tika-<random>.mp4`, not the original attacker filename. 4. `ExternalParser` replaces `${INPUT}` with `stream.getFile().getPath()` (`tika-core/src/main/java/org/apache/tika/parser/external/ExternalParser.java:296-299`) and executes `Runtime.getRuntime().exec(cmd)` with an argument array (`:311-315`). The bundled commands are fixed config strings such as `ffmpeg -i ${INPUT}`, `env FOO=${OUTPUT} exiftool ${INPUT}`, and `env FOO=${OUTPUT} sox --info ${INPUT}` (`tika-core/src/main/resources/org/apache/tika/parser/external/tika-external-parsers.xml:29,53,69`). 5.   "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The sink is real: `TesseractOCRParser#doOCR` builds `cmd` and calls `new ProcessBuilder(cmd)` at `TesseractOCRParser.java:417-437`. However, this report is a false positive because the reported sources do not give the attacker meaningful control over the executed command line in the CWE-078 sense. The reported endpoint parameters are primarily uploaded `InputStream` values (and one `TikaInputStream` URL/input stream path). Those values are not inserted into the command text; they are first spooled into server-generated temporary files and only the temp file paths are passed to Tesseract. In `parse(...)`, Tika calls `tikaStream.getPath()` and creates `tmpOCROutputFile` itself (`TesseractOCRParser.java:264-272`). `TikaInputStream#getPath()` creates a temp file via `TemporaryResources#createTempFile` (`TikaInputStream.java:725-752`, `TemporaryResources.java:86-90`), and the suffix derived from metadata is reduced to a short safe extension only (`FilenameUtils.java:121-131`). So user-controlled document bytes do not become executable command fragments. Separately, even request-controlled OCR options are bounded: `TikaResource.processHeaderConfig` rejects untrusted setter names and illegal characters (`TikaResource.java:268-280`), and `TesseractOCRConfig` validates `language`, `pageSegMode`, `pageSeparator`, and `outputType` (`TesseractOCRConfig.java:153-160, 175-180, 201-212, 306-317`). Dangerous values such as executable path overrides and `trustedPageSeparator` are intentionally blocked in server mode. Because the process is launched with `ProcessBuilder(List<String>)`, not through a shell, and because the attacker cannot control the executable or inject extra argv entries, this is not a practically exploitable command injection.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "B1"
  ],
  "evidence": "Key code path: `cmd = [getTesseractPath()+getTesseractProg(), input.getPath(), output.getPath(), \"--psm\", config.getPageSegMode(), ...]` and then `new ProcessBuilder(cmd)` (`TesseractOCRParser.java:417-437`). The `input` and `output` files are server-created: `tikaStream.getPath(); File tmpOCROutputFile = tmp.createTemporaryFile();` (`TesseractOCRParser.java:264-272`). `TikaInputStream#getPath()` uses `tmp.createTempFile(suffix)` and `TemporaryResources#createTempFile` uses `Files.createTempFile(\"apache-tika-\", actualSuffix)` (`TikaInputStream.java:732`, `TemporaryResources.java:86-90`). The suffix is sanitized to a simple extension only by `FilenameUtils.getSuffixFromPath` (`FilenameUtils.java:121-131`). Server-side guards also exist: `checkTrustWorthy` rejects setters containing `trusted` and rejects illegal header characters (`TikaResource.java:268-280`); `setLanguage` rejects invalid values, with tests explicitly rejecting `\"rm -rf *\"` (`TesseractOCRConfig.java:153-160`, `TesseractOCRConfigTest.java:111-125`); `setPageSegMode` only allows `0-13`; `setPageSeparator` only allows a strict character set; `setOutputType` only allows `txt`/`hocr`. Existing tests confirm the intended blocking behavior: `X-Tika-OCRtesseractPath` with `C://tmp//hello.bat\\0` or `bogus path` returns HTTP 400, and `X-Tika-OCRtrustedPageSeparator` also returns HTTP 400 (`TikaResourceTest.java:474-512`).",
